items.q: How do you say 'his sister' and 'her sister' in French?
items.correct: Both are 'sa sœur' - possessives agree with the OBJECT possessed, not the owner.
items.misconception: His = son, her = sa - based on owner's gender.
items.why: Students apply English logic where 'his/her' shows owner's gender. In French, the possessive agrees with what is owned: sœur is feminine, so SA regardless of whether owner is male or female. Context shows whose.

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: When is la Fete de la Musique?
items.why: It's 21 June. “14 July” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Whereas. “Because” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was French gastronomy added to UNESCO heritage?
items.why: Exact answer: 2010. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
